title: NA-ULIP
word_text:
ULIPs Made Simple: One Plan, Two Powerful Benefits
What is ULIP?
Feeling confused by ULIP? You're not alone. A Unit Linked Insurance Plan (ULIP) is a life insurance policy that provides a combination of risk cover and investment. It gives you protection as well as the possibility of financial growth. Part of your premium in a ULIP is used for life insurance, and the rest is put into the market, including debt, equity, and balanced funds. The policyholder bears the investment risk, and the value of your investment is correlated with the performance of these funds. ULIPs are flexible in that you can choose to switch between funds depending on your objective of investment objective and risk tolerance.
ULIP is not a Mutual Fund
ULIPs and mutual funds might look similar because both link your money to market-based options, but they are quite different in purpose and features. Here is a brief explanation to help you understand ULIP if you're confused and believe it to be the same as a mutual fund.
Key Differences Between ULIPs and Mutual Funds
ULIPs provide life insurance along with investment benefits. Mutual funds do not offer life cover.
ULIPs have a mandatory 5-year lock-in period. Mutual funds are generally liquid except for tax-saving funds such as ELSS (3-year lock-in).
ULIPs provide you with the option to shift between equity, debt, or balanced funds. In mutual funds, investment decisions are controlled by the fund manager.
Premiums paid on ULIPs could be eligible for tax deduction under Section 80C and maturity under Section 10(10D), subject to conditions. Mutual funds do not provide a tax advantage, except for ELSS. However, some capital gains from mutual funds may be taxed at concessional rates.
Each option suits different financial goals and needs.
Types of Funds in ULIPs
ULIPs provide a variety of funds according to your tolerance for market risk. Each type gives you the flexibility to choose where your money goes. This makes ULIPs suitable for people with different goals and comfort levels.
Equity FundsThese put your money in company shares or equity-based assets. They can give high returns, but they also carry higher risk. They are suitable for those who are okay with medium to high risk.
Debt FundsThese make investments in fixed-return instruments such as bank securities or government bonds. They offer consistent, moderate growth and generally carry lower risk.
Hybrid FundsThese combine debt and equity into a single plan. You can choose how much to put in each. This reduces risk while still offering growth. Hybrid funds are good for those who want balance.
You can switch between these funds based on your goals. Many prefer equity early on, then move to debt later.
Know your ULIP
A Unit Linked Insurance Plan gives you life cover along with a chance to grow your money through market-linked funds. You pay a regular amount, and part of it goes toward life protection while the rest is placed in funds like equity, debt, or both. Some ULIP plans offer loyalty additions and fund boosters after staying invested for a 5 or more years. These benefits help enhance the overall value of your plan over time.
Benefits and Features of a ULIP Plan
A Unit Linked Insurance Plan or ULIP is a type of life insurance that also allows you to grow your money. It splits the amount you pay into two parts—one for life cover and the other for investments. You can choose how the investment part is used depending on your financial goals and how much risk you are okay with.
Dual benefit: Life insurance cover and investment in market-linked funds
Flexibility: You can switch between equity, debt, or balanced funds during the policy term
Tax benefits: The premiums you pay may be eligible for deductions under section 80C of the Income Tax Act. If conditions are met, the maturity amount may also be tax-free under section 10(10D)
Partial withdrawals: If necessary, you may withdraw a portion of your funds following the five-year lock-in.
Additional top-ups: To raise the value of your fund, some plans let you gradually add more money.
The difference between the sum assured and the fund value
It is important to know what sum assured and fund value mean when you have a ULIP. These terms tell you how much your family might get.
Sum assured: This is the fixed amount promised when you buy the plan. It does not change.
Fund value: This is the amount your investments are worth based on how well your chosen funds are doing
Payout: If the policyholder passes away, the nominee usually receives whichever is higher—sum assured or fund value, based on the plan terms
Different pay-outs
ULIPs offer different ways to receive money when the policy ends or when a claim is made. These help meet different needs.
Lump sum is the full amount paid all at once
Regular income is given in equal monthly parts over a few years
The mixed option is where some money is given right away, and the rest is paid out in monthly installments
These options give you or your family more control over how the money is used.
Tax benefits of ULIPs
ULIPs are known for providing investment, life insurance, and tax benefits. These benefits have restrictions and requirements and are governed by the current income tax laws.
Premiums paid can be claimed as deductions under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, up to the yearly limit allowed.
The maturity amount may be exempt under Section 10(10D), depending on factors like the sum assured and annual premium.
Tax-free switching: Within the ULIP, switching between debt, equity, or balanced funds is exempt from capital gains tax.
If all policy requirements are satisfied, these exemptions are applicable.
Conclusion
If you’ve ever been confused by ULIP, this guide will help you make things clearer. ULIPs combine life protection with the potential to grow your savings over time. From understanding fund types to knowing the charges and benefits, having ULIP explained in simple terms helps you make better decisions. Always check your goals, risk comfort, and plan features before choosing. ULIPs can be flexible, long-term options when understood the right way.
FAQs
1. How do I exit from the ULIP plan?
After five years, you can take out your money from the plan. If you want to leave before five years, the money is kept in a holding account. It is returned after the lock-in period ends. You do not get access right away. Once the time is over, you can take the full amount or just part of it. You can also choose to stay longer if you want.
2. Are ULIP and FD the same?
ULIP and FD are not the same. ULIP gives life cover and puts money in market funds. The value may change over time. FD gives a fixed return and does not change. One can move up and down, the other stays steady. Each does something different. Some people choose one, others choose the other. It depends on what someone wants. No one way works for all. Both are made for different purposes.
3. How to identify ULIP?
ULIP is a type of life insurance plan. It also puts part of your money into market funds. The plan has a lock-in period, and you can move money between fund types. If you see a plan that gives life cover and also shows money value changing with the market, it could be a ULIP. It often gives choices like picking where your money goes or changing it later based on available options.
4. Can I stop ULIP after 3 years?
You may stop paying after 3 years, but the money stays locked in until 5 years. During this time, you can’t take it out. Some plan features may pause if payments stop. After the lock-in ends, you can take the money or keep it going. If you want to restart payments, there are ways to do it. The rules are different for each plan, so what happens next depends on how the plan works.
5. Is ULIP tax-free after 5 years?
ULIP may give tax benefits if certain rules are followed. After 5 years, the money you get may be tax-free, but only if your premium and plan details meet tax law rules. There are limits on how much you pay and how the plan is set up. These rules can change anytime. So, whether the final amount is tax-free or not depends on what the law says at the time and how the plan works.
6. Is ULIP high risk?
The risk in ULIP depends on how the money is used. Some parts go into funds that change with the market. These can go up or down. That’s called higher risk. Other parts can be placed in steadier places. These are lower risk. You can also move between options. That means risk can be more or less. It’s not one type of risk for everyone. It depends on how the plan is set.
7. What is the difference between PPF or ULIP?
PPF and ULIP are made for different goals. PPF gives fixed returns and is backed by the government. ULIP gives life cover and puts money into market funds. One is slow and steady. The other may go up or down. They don’t work the same way. Each serves different financial goals and comes with distinct features and rules. The better choice depends on what someone needs, not on which is better overall.
8. Is ULIP good for 5 years?
A ULIP needs to be held for at least five years. That’s the lock-in time. You can’t take your money out before that. After five years, you can take it out or let it stay longer. Some plans may offer more value if you keep them for a longer time. The five years is the minimum, but some people keep it for longer. What happens depends on how long the plan runs and its terms.
9. What happens to ULIP after maturity?
When a ULIP ends, that’s called maturity. At that time, the total money value from the plan is given back. You can take it all at once or in parts, depending on how the plan works. The life cover also ends after this. No more payments are needed. It means the plan is finished. What you get depends on how the plan was managed and how the chosen funds performed over the years.
10. Can I withdraw my ULIP?
After the 5-year lock-in period, you can take out some money from your ULIP. This is called a partial withdrawal. It’s allowed in most plans after that time. If you try to take it out before 5 years, the money is held until the lock-in ends. Some plans may also have limits on how often you can take money out. What you can do depends on how the plan is set up.
Disclaimer:
IN THIS POLICY, THE INVESTMENT RISK IN INVESTMENT PORTFOLIO IS BORNE BY THE POLICYHOLDER
The Unit Linked Insurance Products do not offer any liquidity during the first five years of the contract. The policyholder will not be able to surrender or withdraw the monies invested in Unit Linked Insurance Products completely or partially till the end of the fifth year.
ULIPs are different from the traditional insurance products and are subject to the risk factors. The premium paid in ULIPs are subject to investment risks associated with capital markets and the NAVs of the units may go up or down based on the performance of fund and factors influencing the capital market and the insured is responsible for his/her decisions. Please know the associated risks and the applicable charges, from your Insurance agent or the Intermediary or policy document issued by the insurance company. The various funds offered under this contract are the names of the funds and do not in any way indicate the quality of these plans, their future prospects and returns.
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. full_pdf_text:
ULIPs Made Simple: One Plan, Two Powerful Benefits
What is ULIP?
Feeling confused by ULIP? You're not alone. A Unit Linked Insurance Plan (ULIP) is a life insurance policy that provides a combination of life cover and market linked investment. It gives you protection as well as the possibility of financial growth. Part of your premium in a ULIP is used for life insurance, and the rest is put into the market linked funds such as debt, equity, and balanced funds. The policyholder bears the investment risk, and the value of your investment is correlated with the performance of these funds. ULIPs are flexible i.e. you can choose to switch between funds depending on your objective of investment and risk tolerance.
How ULIPs Differ from Mutual Funds?
ULIPs and mutual funds might look similar because both link your money to market linked funds , but they are quite different in purpose and features. Here is a brief explanation to help you understand ULIP if you're confused and believe it to be the same as a mutual fund.
Key Differences Between ULIPs and Mutual Funds
● ULIPs provide life insurance along with market linked investment benefits. Mutual funds do not offer life cover. ● ULIPs have a mandatory 5-year lock-in period. While not all Mutual funds have a lock in period but funds such as ELSS have a 3-year lock-in. ● ULIPs let you move your money between different fund types, like equity or debt, within the same plan. In mutual funds, you cannot switch like this—you need to redeem and invest in a new fund to change your allocation. ● Premiums paid towards ULIPs are eligible for tax deduction under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act. Mutual funds do not generally provide tax deductions on investments, except for Equity Linked Savings Schemes (ELSS), which qualify for deduction under Section 80C up to ₹1.5 lakh per year.
Each option suits different financial goals and needs.
Types of Funds in ULIPs
ULIPs provide a variety of funds according to your tolerance for market risk. Each type gives you the flexibility to choose where your money goes. This makes ULIPs suitable for people with different goals and comfort levels.
1. Equity Funds
These put your money in equity shares of a company or equity-based assets. They can give high returns, but they also carry higher risk. They are suitable for those who are okay with medium to high risk. 2. Debt Funds
These make investments in fixed-return instruments such as corporate bonds or government securities . They offer consistent, moderate growth and generally carry lower risk. 3. Hybrid Funds
These combine debt and equity into a single plan. You can choose how much to put in each. This reduces risk while still offering growth. Hybrid funds are good for those who want balance.
You can switch between these funds based on your goals. Many prefer equity early on, then move to debt later.
Know your ULIP
A Unit Linked Insurance Plan gives you life cover along with a chance to grow your money through market-linked funds. You pay a regular amount known as the premium, part of it goes toward life protection while the rest is placed in funds like equity, debt, or both. Some ULIP plans offer loyalty additions or fund boosters depending on the plans and insurer. These benefits help enhance the overall value of your plan over time.
Benefits and Features of a ULIP Plan
A Unit Linked Insurance Plan or ULIP is a type of life insurance that also allows you to grow your money. It splits your premium into two parts—one for life cover and the other for market linked investments. You can choose how the investment part is used depending on your financial goals and your risk appetite .
● Dual benefit: Life insurance cover and investment in market-linked funds ● Flexibility: You can switch between equity, debt, or balanced funds during the policy term ● Tax benefits: The premiums you pay may be eligible for deductions under section 80C of the Income Tax Act. If conditions are met, the maturity amount may also be tax-free under section 10(10D) ● Partial withdrawals: If necessary, you may withdraw a portion of your funds following the five- year lock-in, subject to policy terms and conditions. ● Additional top-ups: Some ULIP plans offer a top-up facility that lets you add extra money to your policy over time, helping you grow your fund value.
The difference between the sum assured and the fund value
It is important to know what sum assured and fund value mean when you have a ULIP. These terms tell you how much your family might get.
● Sum assured: This is the fixed amount promised when you buy the plan. It does not change. ● Fund value: This is the amount your investments are worth based on how well your chosen funds are doing ● Payout: If the life assured passes away, the nominee usually receives whichever is higher—sum assured or fund value, based on the plan terms
Different pay-outs
ULIPs offer different ways to receive money when the policy ends or when a claim is made. These help meet different financial needs.
● Lump sum is the full amount paid all at once ● Regular income is given in equal monthly parts over a few years ● The mixed option is where some money is given right away, and the rest is paid out in monthly installments
These options give you or your family more control over how the money is used.
Tax benefits of ULIPs
ULIPs are known for providing life insurance, market linked investments and tax benefits.
● Premiums paid can be claimed as deductions under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, up to the yearly limit allowed. ● The maturity amount may be exempt under Section 10(10D), depending on factors like the sum assured and annual premium. ● Tax-free switching: Within the ULIP, switching between debt, equity, or balanced funds is exempt from capital gains tax.
Conclusion
If you’ve ever been confused by ULIP, this guide will help you make things clearer. ULIPs combine life protection with the potential to grow your savings over time. From understanding fund types to knowing the charges and benefits, having ULIP explained in simple terms helps you make better decisions. Always check your goals, risk appetite, and plan features before choosing. ULIPs can be flexible, long-term options when understood the right way.
FAQs
1. How do I exit from the ULIP plan?
. If you decide to exit or leave your ULIP before 5 years, you cannot access your money immediately. After some deductions, your money is moved to a special account called the Discontinued Policy Fund (DP Fund). After 5 years, you can access the funds from this account.
2. Are ULIP and FD the same?
ULIP and FD are not the same. ULIP gives life cover and puts money in market linked funds. The fund value or Net Asset Value (NAV) may change over time. FD gives a fixed, predictable return with no life cover, and your returns do not change. The choice depends on whether you want insurance and growth potential (ULIP) or safety and certainty (FD).
3. How to identify a ULIP?
ULIP is a type of life insurance plan. It also puts part of your money into market linked funds. The plan has a lock-in period, and you can switch between fund types.—such as equity or debt—based on your risk-taking capacity, financial goals, and comfort with market ups and downs. This flexibility helps you align your investments with your personal preferences and changing needs.
4. Can I stop ULIP after 3 years?
You cannot fully stop a ULIP after 3 years, as all ULIPs have a mandatory 5-year lock-in period set by IRDAI regulations. If you stop paying premiums after 3 years, your policy moves to a Discontinued Policy fund (DP Fund), and you cannot access your money until the lock-in period ends. You have a 2-year revival period from the date of discontinuance, to restart the policy by paying overdue premiums.
5. Is ULIP tax-free after 5 years?
ULIP may give tax benefits if certain rules are followed. After 5 years, the money you get may be tax- free, but only if your premium and plan details meet tax law rules. There are limits on how much you pay and how the plan is set up. These rules can change anytime. So, whether the final amount is tax-free or not depends on what law is prevailing at that point of time and how the plan works.
6. Is ULIP high risk?
A ULIP lets you choose how your money is invested. If you pick equity funds, your money goes into shares, which can move up or down quickly, this means higher risk. If you pick debt funds, your money goes into things like bonds, which are usually more steady and less risky. You can switch between these options. So, the risk in a ULIP depends on where you put your money, your comfort with ups and downs, and your financial plans. The risk is not the same for everyone, it changes based on your choices.
7. What is the difference between PPF or ULIP?
PPF and ULIP are made for different goals. PPF gives fixed returns and are backed by the government. ULIP gives life cover along with market linked investments. Each serves different financial goals and comes with distinct features and rules. The better choice depends on what someone needs,.
8. Is ULIP a suitable plan for 5 years?
A ULIP offers both insurance and market-linked investment in one plan, with a mandatory lock-in period of 5 years. This means you must stay invested for at least 5 years before you can access your money or make withdrawals. ULIPs are structured to encourage long-term financial planning and may suit individuals who are comfortable with a 5-year commitment. The value of your investment will depend on market performance and the funds you choose. ULIPs also provide flexibility and tax benefits during this period.
9. What happens to ULIP after maturity?
When a ULIP ends, that’s called maturity. At that time, the total money value from the plan is given back. You can take it all at once or in parts, depending on how the plan works. The life cover also ends after this. What you get depends on how the chosen funds performed over the years.
Disclaimer:

The policyholder will not be able to surrender or withdraw the monies invested in Unit Linked Insurance Products completely or partially till the end of the fifth year. ULIPs are different from the traditional insurance products and are subject to the risk factors. The premium paid in ULIPs are subject to investment risks associated with capital markets and the NAVs of the units may go up or down based on the performance of fund and factors influencing the capital market and the insured is responsible for his/her decisions. Please know the associated risks and the applicable charges, from your Insurance agent or the Intermediary or policy document issued by the insurance company. changes.pdf_final: Always check your goals, risk appetite, and plan features before choosing.

changes.word_original: After five years, you can take out your money from the plan.
If you want to leave before five years, the money is kept in a holding account.
changes.pdf_final: If you decide to exit or leave your ULIP before 5 years, you cannot access your money immediately.

changes.word_original: It is returned after the lock-in period ends.
changes.pdf_final: After some deductions, your money is moved to a special account called the Discontinued Policy Fund (DP Fund).

changes.word_original: You do not get access right away.
changes.pdf_final: After 5 years, you can access the funds from this account.

changes.word_original: Once the time is over, you can take the full amount or just part of it.

changes.word_original: ULIP gives life cover and puts money in market funds.
changes.pdf_final: ULIP gives life cover and puts money in market linked funds.

changes.word_original: The value may change over time.
changes.pdf_final: The fund value or Net Asset Value (NAV) may change over time.

changes.word_original: FD gives a fixed return and does not change.
changes.pdf_final: FD gives a fixed, predictable return with no life cover, and your returns do not change.

changes.word_original: One can move up and down, the other stays steady.
changes.pdf_final: The choice depends on whether you want insurance and growth potential (ULIP) or safety and certainty (FD).

changes.word_original: Each does something different.

changes.word_original: Some people choose one, others choose the other.

changes.word_original: How to identify ULIP?
changes.pdf_final: How to identify a ULIP?

changes.word_original: It also puts part of your money into market funds.
changes.pdf_final: It also puts part of your money into market linked funds.

changes.word_original: The plan has a lock-in period, and you can move money between fund types.
changes.pdf_final: The plan has a lock-in period, and you can switch between fund types.—such as equity or debt—based on your risk-taking capacity, financial goals, and comfort with market ups and downs.

changes.word_original: If you see a plan that gives life cover and also shows money value changing with the market, it could be a ULIP.
changes.pdf_final: This flexibility helps you align your investments with your personal preferences and changing needs.

changes.word_original: It often gives choices like picking where your money goes or changing it later based on available options.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: You may stop paying after 3 years, but the money stays locked in until 5 years.
changes.pdf_final: You cannot fully stop a ULIP after 3 years, as all ULIPs have a mandatory 5-year lock-in period set by IRDAI regulations.

changes.word_original: During this time, you can’t take it out.
changes.pdf_final: If you stop paying premiums after 3 years, your policy moves to a Discontinued Policy fund (DP Fund), and you cannot access your money until the lock-in period ends.

changes.word_original: Some plan features may pause if payments stop.
changes.pdf_final: You have a 2-year revival period from the date of discontinuance, to restart the policy by paying overdue premiums.

changes.word_original: After the lock-in ends, you can take the money or keep it going.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If you want to restart payments, there are ways to do it.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: The rules are different for each plan, so what happens next depends on how the plan works.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: After 5 years, the money you get may be tax-free, but only if your premium and plan details meet tax law rules.
changes.pdf_final: After 5 years, the money you get may be tax- free, but only if your premium and plan details meet tax law rules.

changes.word_original: So, whether the final amount is tax-free or not depends on what the law says at the time and how the plan works.
changes.pdf_final: So, whether the final amount is tax-free or not depends on what law is prevailing at that point of time and how the plan works.

changes.word_original: The risk in ULIP depends on how the money is used.
changes.pdf_final: A ULIP lets you choose how your money is invested.

changes.word_original: Some parts go into funds that change with the market.
changes.pdf_final: If you pick equity funds, your money goes into shares, which can move up or down quickly, this means higher risk.

changes.word_original: These can go up or down.
changes.pdf_final: If you pick debt funds, your money goes into things like bonds, which are usually more steady and less risky.

changes.word_original: That’s called higher risk.
changes.pdf_final: You can switch between these options.

changes.word_original: Other parts can be placed in steadier places.
changes.pdf_final: So, the risk in a ULIP depends on where you put your money, your comfort with ups and downs, and your financial plans.

changes.word_original: These are lower risk.
changes.pdf_final: The risk is not the same for everyone, it changes based on your choices.

changes.word_original: You can also move between options.

changes.word_original: That means risk can be more or less.

changes.word_original: Is ULIP good for 5 years?
changes.pdf_final: Is ULIP a suitable plan for 5 years?

changes.word_original: A ULIP needs to be held for at least five years.
changes.pdf_final: A ULIP offers both insurance and market-linked investment in one plan, with a mandatory lock-in period of 5 years.

changes.word_original: That’s the lock-in time.
changes.pdf_final: This means you must stay invested for at least 5 years before you can access your money or make withdrawals.

changes.word_original: You can’t take your money out before that.
changes.pdf_final: ULIPs are structured to encourage long-term financial planning and may suit individuals who are comfortable with a 5-year commitment.

changes.word_original: After five years, you can take it out or let it stay longer.
changes.pdf_final: The value of your investment will depend on market performance and the funds you choose.

changes.word_original: Some plans may offer more value if you keep them for a longer time.
changes.pdf_final: ULIPs also provide flexibility and tax benefits during this period.

changes.word_original: The five years is the minimum, but some people keep it for longer.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: What happens depends on how long the plan runs and its terms.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: No more payments are needed.
changes.pdf_final: What you get depends on how the chosen funds performed over the years.

changes.word_original: It means the plan is finished.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: What you get depends on how the plan was managed and how the chosen funds performed over the years.

changes.word_original: Can I withdraw my ULIP?
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: After the 5-year lock-in period, you can take out some money from your ULIP.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: This is called a partial withdrawal.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: It’s allowed in most plans after that time.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If you try to take it out before 5 years, the money is held until the lock-in ends.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Some plans may also have limits on how often you can take money out.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: What you can do depends on how the plan is set up.
